text: The surgeon encounters plastered small bowel adhesions in the pelvis, obscuring the view of the pelvic region. The small bowel loops are visibly stuck along the right iliac vessels, presenting a challenge in the minimally invasive approach.

The surgeon uses robotic technology to lysive adhesions, with a loop of small bowel plastered to the right dome of the bladder and another loop behind it near the left dome of the bladder. The colon is also visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the small bowel to expose the pelvis, avoiding injuries. A cavity is visible, with a rind surrounding it, as the surgeon attempts to reposition a loop of bowel. The medial aspect of the sigmoid colon and additional loops of small bowel are exposed, with pus indicating interloop abscesses not drained by the IR drain.

The surgeon uses cold scissors and blunt pushing to separate a loop of bowel from the surrounding rind, working carefully to avoid injuries. The pelvis remains obscured, indicating the complexity of the colon resection ahead. The dissection proceeds from the bladder flap with minimal use of cautery.

The surgeon separates a loop of small bowel from the rectus sigmoid mesentery at the pelvic brim, revealing the uterus and residual pus from the abscess cavity. The colon is lifted to expose the deeper pelvis and the right ureter, while the surgeon continues to free the remaining small bowel.

The surgeon identifies the cecum, appendix, and terminal ileum, and mobilizes the terminal ileum mesentery. The small bowel is now cleared, allowing a view into the pelvis where a loop of sigmoid colon is flopped down. The colon is visibly adhered to the left dome of the bladder.

The surgeon uses the robotic arm to separate the colon from the uterus, revealing the anterior pelvis. The left tube and ovary are preserved during this process. The IR drain is visible in the posterior left pelvis, showing a well-cleaned abscess area. The uterus is suspended to enhance access and visualization of the pelvic region.

The surgeon navigates to the deep anterior pelvis to identify the true rectum, with the loop of sigmoid colon flopped over the area. The right lateral mesorectum is visible, drawn in and adherent to the anterior portion of the colon. The surgeon uses suction to clear blood from the field, working with four ports positioned in the left upper quadrant, epigastrium, right mid quadrant, and right lower quadrant. The camera is positioned in arm three, allowing the surgeon to operate with two left hands.

The surgeon navigates the deeper pelvis using scissors in arm number four, encountering difficulty as the scissors hit the iliac vessels. The camera is port-hopped to optimize the angle, revealing a loop of colon flopped over the cul-de-sac that has yet to be identified.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection in the pelvis, identifying the left lateral border using the left ureter as a landmark. The left ureter is located and followed down into the pelvis, allowing the rectus sigmoid to be moved off the sidewall and facilitating dissection through an unviolated plane.

The surgeon joins the dissection medially and lifts the mesorectum posteriorly, providing access to the deep pelvis. This maneuver facilitates better manipulation of the bowel that is adhered in the area, allowing for improved visualization and access.

The surgeon identifies the colon adhered to the right lateral mesorectum and begins cutting through the tissue with precision, ensuring not to injure the anterior wall of the rectum. The dissection reveals the anterior rectum, which has been exposed to an abscess cavity, influencing the decision to avoid an anastomosis. The surgeon elevates the rectum further out of the pelvis, separating it anteriorly from the cul-de-sac.

The surgeon mobilizes the remaining colon to facilitate division of the rectum, revealing the anterior wall of the rectum, which appears damaged. A scope is used to confirm no additional colon is flopped over the cul-de-sac. The surgeon marks the site for a colostomy and performs lateral mobilization to ensure adequate reach. A pelvic drain is removed, and a sponge is applied to manage minor bleeding. The mesentery is dissected and joined at two spots, avoiding high ligation. The specimen is extracted through the colostomy site, which will be closed during Hartmann reversal.

The surgeon cleans off the rectal mesentery, ensuring the dissection is below the loop of colon previously flopped over the cul-de-sac. Due to the diseased and adherent sigmoid colon, the surgeon resects just beneath the recto-sigmoid junction, performs a perfusion assessment, and divides the upper rectum.

The surgeon creates a left upper quadrant colostomy at the end of the procedure. The camera is positioned in arm two, allowing the stapler to be used in arm three, resulting in a slightly different view. The rectal staple line is marked with a proline suture to aid future surgical interventions. The surgeon performs a leak test on the rectal stump, ensuring no leakage after anterior dissection near the cul-de-sac.
